senses.definition: A type of adjustable mirror used in telescopes to reflect light into the eyepiece, providing precise alignment without moving the telescope.
examples: The astronomer adjusted the siderostats to center the image.
Siderostats help in fine-tuning the optical path during observations.

senses.definition: The branch of science or philosophy dealing with the use of iron or sideric materials in technology or the manipulation of iron-based processes; historically related to metallurgy and alchemy.
examples: The volume discusses siderotechny as a historical precursor to modern metallurgy.

senses.definition: Containing or relating to iron; iron-bearing; also describing a fossil or mineral with iron content.
examples: Siderotic veins were observed in the ore sample.
The siderotic minerals stained reddish due to iron content.

senses.definition: Containing iron; iron-rich; relating to or containing siderite or iron compounds.
examples: Siderous rocks show a high iron content.
The siderous mineral gleamed with a metallic luster.

senses.definition: A genus of tropical trees in the mahogany family; also refers to wood from these trees used in timber and carpentry.
examples: The shipbuilder used sideroxylon timber for the frame.
Sideroxylon is prized for its durability in tropical climates.

senses.definition: People who reside at or on the side of something; in card games, players on the sides of a table; in contexts, supporters or opposers placed on the sidelines.
examples: The observers sat with the sid ers along the outer boundary.
Two siders from the opposing team argued from the sidelines.

senses.definition: The state or role of being a sidecar driver or side in a vehicle; figuratively, the position or role alongside another person or thing.
examples: He joined the sidership of the project as an advisor.
The sidership of the team required close collaboration.

senses.definition: Relating to the processing or treatment of iron and steel; pertaining to iron metallurgy and steelmaking.
examples: The siderurgical industry drives much of the region's economy.
Siderurgical techniques have evolved with modern steel plants.

senses.definition: The science or study of iron and steel metallurgy; the production and processing of iron and steel.
examples: He researched siderurgy to improve alloy composition.
Siderurgy covers ore smelting, refining, and alloy creation.

senses.definition: The surfaces at the left, right, or any edge of an object; one part or aspect of something.
examples: Cut the cake into two equal sides.
The sides of the box are made of cardboard.

senses.definition: A saddle designed to be used while riding with the rider seated on the side of the horse rather than astride.
examples: The rider mounted the horse in a sidesaddle for the parade.
She learned the skill of sidesaddle riding.

senses.definition: Saddles designed for riding on the side of a horse; plural of sidesaddle.
examples: Museum pieces include several antique sidesaddles.
The sidesaddles on display illustrate 19th-century equestrian fashion.

senses.definition: A strong shaking or jolt to the side; a side-to-side movement.
examples: The car gave a sudden sideshake as it hit the pothole.

senses.definition: A shot or blow directed to the side; a shot taken from the side in sports or hunting.
examples: The photographer took a sideshot of the crowd.
In marksmanship, a sideshot can be more difficult to aim.

senses.definition: Plural of sideshot; multiple shots taken from the side in sports or photography; also shoots that branch off at the side of a main stem in botany.
examples: The photographer captured several sideshoots during the event.
In botany, the plant produces several sideshoots from the main stem.

senses.definition: A small, secondary attraction or display accompanying a main event; a minor show or entertainment.
examples: The convention had a few side shows in the hall.
The magician performed a quick sideshow between acts.

senses.definition: Multiple small attractions or entertainments that accompany a main event.
examples: The fair had several sideshows and a big main arena.
They wandered from one sideshow to another.

senses.definition: A slide to the side; a slipping movement sideways.
examples: The car went into a sideslip on the wet road.
A sideslip can be dangerous at high speeds.

senses.definition: Past tense of sideslip; moved sideways in a slipping manner.
examples: The vehicle sideslipped for a moment before correcting course.

senses.definition: Present participle of sideslip; sliding sideways.
examples: The car is sideslipping on the icy road.

senses.definition: Multiple instances of slipping sideways; detours or evasive movements to the side.
examples: The skid marks show several abrupt sideslips.

senses.definition: A person who acts as a spokesman or representative on one side of an issue; a participant who supports one side in a negotiation or trial.
examples: The lawyer asked the sidesman to present the key argument.
In debates, a sidesman often handles evidence from their side.

senses.definition: Plural of sidesman; multiple representatives or spokespeople on one side.
examples: The sidesmen presented a united front during the negotiation.

senses.definition: A spin around a vertical axis given to a shot or object from the side to influence its trajectory; in aviation, a yaw motion around the vertical axis.
examples: The pitcher's fastball had a noticeable sidespin.
The drone angle included a slight sidespin to stabilize the flight.

senses.definition: Multiple instances of sidespin; repeated lateral spins, especially in sports or ball trajectory contexts.
examples: The batter relied on sidespins to curve the ball into the strike zone.
